paper:
The fusiform face area: a cortical region
specialized for the perception of faces
Hypothesis: FFA is a domain-specific area, rather than an area that deals with individual differences or general expert recognition.
Reported Statistics: "...the FFA response to faces was about three times as strong as the FFA response to houses..."
Robustness Check: Task manipulation check — no significant effect of task [F(1,6) < 1] and no task × stimulus interaction [F(3,18) < 1]

paper: The fusiform face area subserves face perception, not generic within-category identification
Hypothesis: The FFA is involved in both the detection and identification of faces, but it is not involved in the within-category identification of non-face objects (even for objects of expertise).

paper: Sparsely-distributed organization of face and limb activations in human ventral temporal cortex
Hypothesis: They propose a sparsely-distributed organization of the ventral temporal cortex (VTC). They hypothesize that face- and limb-selective activations alternate in a series of largely non-overlapping clusters (mFus, pFus, and IOG for faces) that maintain a consistent spatial relationship to each other and to retinotopic landmarks.
Robustness Check: Replication across 3 independent experiments and 2 sessions

paper: The Role of Experience in the Face-Selective Response in Right FFA
Hypothesis: To test whether individual differences in FFA’s selectivity for faces relate to differences in behavioral face-recognition ability and to investigate the role of experience in this brain-behavior correlation.
Dependent Variable: Beta Weights (Regression parameter weights calculated for relevant stimulus categories).

paper: Revisiting the Role of the Fusiform Face Area in Visual Expertise
Hypothesis: The FFA is involved in processing any class of visual stimuli that share the same basic configuration and for which the observer has gained substantial visual expertise (Expertise Hypothesis).
Robustness Check: explicit replication of Gauthier et al., 2000, with methodological improvements

paper: Interaction of Face and Voice Areas during Speaker Recognition
Hypothesis: The fusiform face region is coupled with the superior temporal sulcus voice region during familiar speaker recognition.
Statistics Type: Statistical Parametric Mapping software (T-tests or F-tests)

paper: Mental Imagery of Faces and Places Activates Corresponding Stimulus-Specific Brain Regions
Hypothesis: FFA or PPA are selective activated during imagery of faces or places.
Statistics Type: a signed Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test, correcting for linear drift and using a 1-2-1 Hanning kernel
Effect Size: % signal change: faces perception ≈ 1.94–2.01% vs. places ≈ 0.72–0.76% in FFA (Experiment 2 magnitude data, Table 2)
Robustness Check: Replication (Experiment 2 replicates FFA selectivity; individual-subject results shown for N=8 in Figure 2)

paper: Cortical Face-Selective Responses Emerge Early in Human Infancy
Hypothesis: Cortical face selectivity in the FFA (VTC) is present in the first year of human infancy (specifically ages 2.6–9.6 months).
Independent Variable: faces vs. bodies, objects, and scenes
Effect Size: Partial η² ≈ 0.189 ( computed as [F × df_num] / [F × df_num + df_den] = 24.15 / 127.58)
Robustness Check: Cross-validation (leave-one-subrun-out fROI procedure) + weighted LME sensitivity analysis

paper: The fusiform face area responds equivalently to faces and abstract shapes in the left and central visual fields
Hypothesis: The FFA may be particularly tuned to shapes presented in the left visual field (LVF) and central visual field (CVF) rather than being specialized solely for holistic face processing.

paper: Separate Face and Body Selectivity on the Fusiform Gyrus
Hypothesis: The apparent dual selectivity of both the FFA and the FBA for both faces and bodies may result from blurring of the responses from two adjacent but distinct cortical regions, one selectively responsive only for faces and the other only for bodies.

paper: The Fusiform Face Area: A Module in Human Extrastriate Cortex Specialized for Face Perception
Hypothesis: The fusiform face area responds more strongly to faces than non-face objects
Independent Variable: Faces vs Objects, Scrambled faces, houses, hands
Dependent Variable: BOLD/MR signal intensity in FFA / right fusiform gyrus ROI
Effect Size: Selectivity ratio (faces/objects % signal change) = 2.8; partial η² inferrable from F(1,4) = 27.1-> partial η² ≈ .87

paper: Perception of Face Parts and Face Configurations: An fMRI Study Available
link: Perception of Face Parts and Face Configurations: An fMRI Study | Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience | MIT Press
Hypothesis: The FFA is sensitive to both face parts and face configuration, whereas the OFA and fSTS are sensitive only to face parts.

paper: The Fusiform Face Area Is Engaged in Holistic, Not Parts-Based, Representation of Faces
Hypothesis: The FFA is engaged in holistic representation of faces, meaning featural information is combined with (not independent of) configural information.
Dependent Variable: Neural: Correlation of spatial patterns of activation (MVPA);

paper: Properties of face localizer activations and their application in functional magnetic resonance imaging fingerprinting
Hypothesis: Fusiform face areas show stronger responses to faces than non-face control stimuli
Design: Human fMRI, within-subject block design face-localizer
Independent Variable: Faces vs. houses, objects, and landscapes
Sample Size: 80 in the main one-scan group; also 5 additional participants scanned 5 times for reproducibility analyses

paper: Face Perception: Domain Specific, Not Process Specific
link: Moving and Static Faces, Bodies, Objects, and Scenes Are Differentially Represented across the Three Visual Pathways - PMC
Hypothesis: The FFA responds more strongly to faces than houses
Dependent Variable: Percent signal change / BOLD response in right and left FFA
Sample Size: 15 scanned; 14 included in the main bilateral FFA ANOVA because 14 showed both right and left FFA
Robustness Check: Cluster-level correction (3dClustSim) applied to whole-brain maps; individual-level ROI replication reported

paper: High-resolution imaging reveals highly selective nonface clusters in the fusiform face area
Hypothesis: FFA has a reliable heterogeneous structure: localized subregions within the FFA highly selective to faces are spatially interdigitated with localized subregions highly selective to different object categories
Effect Size: Mean selectivity index for face-preferring voxels = 0.4 ± 0.2
Robustness Check: Cross-validation (odd/even run split across independent image sets)

paper: Process- and Domain-Specificity in Regions Engaged for Face Processing: An fMRI Study of Perceptual Differentiation.
Hypothesis: The right fusiform face area (FFA) responds more strongly to faces than houses
Independent Variable: Faces vs Objects, Faces vs texture
Dependent Variable: fMRI signal / BOLD response in the right FFA

paper: Distinct response properties between the FFA to faces and the PPA to houses
Hypothesis: FFA responds more strongly to faces than houses
Dependent Variable: BOLD response in face-selective FFA ROIs
Robustness Check: Correction for multiple comparisons; MVPA cross-validation

paper: Detecting faces in pure noise images: a functional MRI study on top-down perception
Hypothesis: Positive correlations were found for the degree of greater face-detection activation between the fusiform face area and bilateral inferior frontal gyri.
Robustness Check: FFA ROI independently localised via separate localizer task

paper: The effect of face inversion on the human fusiform face area
Hypothesis: Does the FFA respond to faces or does it respond to face confounding areas
Independent Variable: upright greyscale vs invered greyscale faces

paper:
Defining face perception areas in the human brain: A large-scale factorial fMRI
face localizer analysis
Hypothesis: To identify and define the set of face-preferential areas in the human brain (FFA, OFA, pSTS, etc.) using a large-scale factorial fMRI localizer to assess their sensitivity to face-specific information vs. low-level visual cues.
Robustness Check: FDR correction + cross-validation with independent data

paper: Internal and External Features of the Face Are Represented Holistically in Face-Selective Regions of Visual Cortex
Hypothesis: The face's internal and external features are represented holistically in face-selective regions of the human brain.

paper: Activation of the middle fusiform 'face area' increases with expertise in recognizing novel objects
Hypothesis: Is the "face-area" an expertise area?
Dependent Variable: Sum of t-values within 8×8 voxel ROI for upright minus inverted sequential-matching contrast

paper: Greater sensitivity of the cortical face processing system to perceptually-equated face detection
Hypothesis: FFA and OFA are more sensitive to face detection signals than to non-face object (tree) detection signals, and therefore selectively support the initial process of face vs. non-face object perception.

paper: Individuating Faces and Common Objects Produces Equal Responses in Putative Face-Processing Areas in the Ventral Occipitotemporal Cortex
Hypothesis: The FFA and OFA are not face-specific; they respond to the processing demand of individuation (discriminating between specific exemplars within a category). Under individuation tasks, faces and non-face objects should produce equal responses.
Robustness Check: Correction for multiple comparisons - Bonferroni-corrected paired t-tests

paper: Response Properties of the Human Fusiform Face Area
Hypothesis: FFA responds more strongly to faces (human/cat/schematic) than to non-face objects
Independent Variable: human faces vs. cat faces vs. schematic faces vs. objects

paper: High-resolution imaging of expertise reveals reliable object selectivity in the fusiform face area related to perceptual performance
Hypothesis: The FFA is not exclusively face-specific but part of a network tuned by experience; HR-fMRI will show car expertise effects even within the most face-selective voxels, proving it is not due to spatial blurring.
Design: Mixed / Correlational (Within-subject stimulus categories; between-subject individual differences in car expertise correlated with BOLD response).
Robustness Check: Cross-validation: voxels were sorted on odd runs and responses measured on even runs (and vice versa), then averaged.
